sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: MOUSE(row=21, col=39).
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 2, level 1.
Valid actions right now: MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 1 of about 22 target actions so far.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content: Good progress! Clicking the purple ring caused it to move down along the path. The animation shows it moved from the ring position toward the white diamond. Let me check the current state to see exactly where everything is now.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Check what changed after clicking the purple ring
seg = current_frame.segmentation
nodes = seg['nodes']

# Find the purple ring and other key objects
by_color = {}
for n in nodes:
    c = n['color']
    if c not in by_color:
        by_color[c] = []
    by_color[c].append(n)

print("=== Key objects ===")
# Purple objects
for n in by_color.get('p', []):
    b = n['boundary']
    if n['pixels'] > 1:
        print(f"  p: id={n['id']}, px={n['pixels']}, boundary={b}")
    else:
        print(f"  p: id={n['id']}, px=1, at {b[0]}")

# White diamond
for n in by_color.get('W', []):
    if n['pixels'] > 1:
        b = n['boundary']
        rows = [p[0] for p in b]
        cols = [p[1] for p in b]
        print(f"  W: id={n['id']}, px={n['pixels']}, center~({sum(rows)//len(rows)},{sum(cols)//len(cols)})")

# Magenta
for n in by_color.get('M', []):
    b = n['boundary']
    print(f"  M: id={n['id']}, px={n['pixels']}, at {b[0] if len(b)==1 else b}")

# Dark gray
for n in by_color.get('G', []):
    if n['pixels'] > 1:
        b = n['boundary']
        rows = [p[0] for p in b]
        cols = [p[1] for p in b]
        print(f"  G: id={n['id']}, px={n['pixels']}, center~({sum(rows)//len(rows)},{sum(cols)//len(cols)})")

# Light gray path
w_nodes = by_color.get('w', [])
print(f"\n  w (light gray) path: {len(w_nodes)} segments")
for n in sorted(w_nodes, key=lambda x: x['id']):
    b = n['boundary']
    print(f"    id={n['id']}, px={n['pixels']}, boundary={b}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Interesting! The purple ring has moved down. Let me understand what happened:
Before:
- Purple ring was at rows 18-24, cols 36-42 (centered at ~21, 39)
- White diamond was at ~36, 7
- Purple square with magenta center was at ~47, 17
- Dark gray diamond was at ~59, 27
After:
- The purple ring (now id=32 with 20 pixels) moved to center ~40, 33 - it's now a filled shape with magenta center at (40, 33)
- There's now a white diamond (id=9, 12 pixels) at center ~(21, 39) - this seems to be where the purple ring WAS
- The light gray path now extends from the old ring position down to the dark gray diamond
Wait, let me reconsider. The purple ring has moved DOWN along the path. It was at ~(21, 39) and is now at ~(40, 33). The white diamond appeared where the ring was.
Actually looking more carefully:
- Node 9 (W, 12 pixels) at center ~(21, 39) - this is a new white diamond where the purple ring was
- Node 32 (p, 20 pixels) at boundary [[38, 32], ...] - this is the purple ring that moved down
- Node 33 (M, 1 pixel) at [40, 33] - magenta center of the moved ring
- The dark gray diamond (G, 12 pixels) is still at ~59, 27
So the purple ring moved from (21, 39) down along the path to around (40, 33). A white diamond appeared at the old position.
The goal seems to be to move the purple ring all the way to the dark gray diamond at the bottom. Let me click the purple ring again to move it further.
The purple ring is now centered around (40, 33). Let me click there.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# The purple ring moved from (21,39) to around (40,33). 
# Click it again to move it further along the path toward the dark gray diamond at (59,27).
# The ring center is approximately at (40, 33)
action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 40, 'col': 33}])
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
